title: Proposed Solutions — Closing the Unrecognized Gap
section: Solution A — Agentic Web Search for Zero-History Products
text:
Solution A — Agentic Web Search for Zero-History Products

The problem
When a product has no purchase history at all, Step 5 has no anchors to compute proximity against — no DBSCAN cluster, no WAP, nothing. The only signal available is the product description itself.


Proposed approach
An agentic pipeline can augment these rows autonomously:

Query construction — from RecognizedProductDesc, RecognizedManufacturerName, and RecognizedProductCatalogNumber build a structured search query (e.g., "Abbott 1192ANS Anchor SwiftLock unit of measure" or a medical-device pricing database lookup).
Search & parse — the agent calls a web search tool or a medical product catalogue API and extracts candidate UOM labels and typical pack sizes.
Citation attachment — every suggestion is stored with its source URL and snippet so an analyst can verify it before accepting.
Confidence flagging — the agent rates its own confidence (e.g., “found exact catalog number” vs. “matched by description keyword”). Low-confidence suggestions are flagged for mandatory human review before write-back.



What needs to be built and tested

Prompt engineering to reliably extract structured UOM data from unstructured web pages.
A de-duplication pass to collapse near-identical suggestions.
A review UI that surfaces the citation alongside the suggested UOM for analyst sign-off.
A feedback loop: accepted suggestions are written back into the purchase history so future runs can resolve the same products without another search.



Trade-offs







Benefit
Risk




Extends coverage to truly novel products
Hallucination / incorrect web data — must require analyst approval


Provides auditable citations
Adds latency and API cost per zero-history product


Self-improving over time as accepted results grow history
Needs prompt fine-tuning per product category

title: Proposed Solutions — Closing the Unrecognized Gap
section: Solution B — Similar-Product Transfer (Purchase History Bridge)
text:
Solution B — Similar-Product Transfer (Purchase History Bridge)

The problem
The 31,400 rows with very low purchase history have some data but not enough to build a reliable DBSCAN cluster or meaningful WAP.


Proposed approach
Rather than going to the web, look inside the existing purchase history for similar products:

Similarity candidates — for a product with sparse history, find other products in the catalogue that share:

Same RecognizedManufacturerID
Same RecognizedProductTypeID
Similar description tokens (keyword overlap or embedding distance)

UOM profile transfer — the similar product’s ThresholdV2 clusters and WAP are borrowed as a prior. The proximity scoring in Step 5 runs exactly as normal, just with the borrowed thresholds substituting for the absent ones.
Downgraded confidence — because the thresholds are borrowed, the resulting confidence score is multiplied by a transfer discount factor (e.g., 0.80) to signal to downstream consumers that this is an inferred — not directly observed — assignment.
Analyst review trigger — rows resolved via transfer are surfaced in a separate review queue so analysts can confirm the analogy is valid for that product category.



What needs to be discussed with analysts

Acceptable similarity criteria — does same manufacturer + same product type constitute a strong enough analogy? Are there categories where this would be systematically misleading (e.g., two products with the same type ID but very different pricing tiers)?
Discount factor calibration — how much should transferred confidence be penalised?
Segment sensitivity — certain clinical categories (e.g., implants vs. consumables) may require different similarity rules.



Trade-offs







Benefit
Risk




Uses existing, trusted purchase data — no external calls
Wrong analogy can propagate a systematic error


Fast — no added latency
Similarity is hard to define without analyst input


Gracefully degrades to unrecognized if no similar product found
Requires a new similarity index to be maintained

title: Proposed Solutions — Closing the Unrecognized Gap
section: Solution C — Threshold Relaxation for Out-of-Range Prices
text:
Solution C — Threshold Relaxation for Out-of-Range Prices

The problem
The 21,900 rows with adequate history fail because their file PriceUOM falls outside every DBSCAN cluster at a distance where the proximity score is below 0.30.
Common causes:

Supplier price change — the vendor raised or lowered prices since the last purchase, shifting the quote outside the historical band.
New pack size — the supplier is now selling in a quantity not previously ordered (e.g., BX/24 when history only has BX/12 and BX/100).
Currency / unit mismatch — a price-per-case was accidentally quoted as price-per-each.



Proposed approaches

C1 — Widened acceptance window (lower LOG_EPS threshold)
LOG_EPS = 0.25 currently corresponds to a roughly 1.8× tolerance from the cluster edge. Increasing it to 0.40 extends tolerance to ~2.5×. This is a global change and must be evaluated on a hold-out set to measure false-positive impact.


C2 — Price-trend adjustment
For products with a time-stamped purchase history, the system can detect upward or downward price trends and project the cluster range forward. A product whose EA price has trended from $10 → $13 → $16 over 12 months is likely priced around $19 today; the cluster built on 12-month-old data will be offset.


C3 — Analyst escalation queue
Rows where the best candidate score is 0.20–0.30 (just below acceptance) are surfaced in a dedicated review queue. The analyst sees the best DBSCAN candidate, its score, and the distance from the cluster edge — making a quick accept/reject decision fast. Accepted overrides are recorded and used to retrain the threshold or expand the cluster.

title: Proposed Solutions — Closing the Unrecognized Gap
text:
Prioritisation Matrix


                            
                                            











Solution
Potential reach
Implementation effort
Requires analyst input




A — Agentic search
47 rows (zero-history)
High — prompt engineering + review UI
Yes — citation sign-off


B — Similar-product transfer
31,400 rows (low history)
Medium — similarity index + discount logic
Yes — similarity criteria definition


C1 — Widen LOG_EPS
Subset of 21,900 rows
Low — one config change + validation
No — but needs hold-out evaluation


C3 — Escalation queue
Near-miss rows (score 0.20–0.30)
Low — UI change only
Yes — per-row review

title: Proposed Solutions — Closing the Unrecognized Gap
text:
Recommended Starting Point

Immediate — implement C3 (escalation queue) to capture near-misses with minimal engineering. This recovers the easiest unrecognized rows and builds analyst feedback data that can be used to calibrate all other solutions.
Short term — evaluate C1 (LOG_EPS relaxation) on a hold-out set. If the false-positive rate is acceptable, deploy it to recover a large portion of the out-of-range rows automatically.
Medium term — design and pilot Solution B (similar-product transfer) with analysts. The similarity criteria need to be co-designed with domain experts before any automated write-back.
Longer term — prototype Solution A (agentic search) on a small batch of zero-history products, evaluate citation quality, and build the review workflow before scaling.

title: Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works and Why It Succeeds
text:
Steps 1–4 rely on hand-crafted or statistically naive min/max price ranges. A single extreme purchase can widen a V1 threshold so much that it matches too broadly — or, when data is sparse, the range is too tight to match realistic new transactions.



DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) groups historical purchase prices for each product into dense clusters while treating statistical outliers as noise. The resulting ThresholdV2 records store:

MinThreshold / MaxThreshold — the actual observed price range of the cluster (not a hand-tuned rule).
QuantityOfEAInUOM — the UOM quantity that cluster represents.
MeanEA — a per-each price anchor used when no WAP entry is available.

Because outliers are excluded, the clusters are tighter and more representative of real analyst expectations. A product that historically sells as a BX/12 at $120–$180 will produce one cluster with that range; a separate cluster covers the EA sales at $10–$15.

title: Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works and Why It Succeeds
text:
Steps 1–4 rely on hand-crafted or statistically naive min/max price ranges. A single extreme purchase can widen a V1 threshold so much that it matches too broadly — or, when data is sparse, the range is too tight to match realistic new transactions.



DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) groups historical purchase prices for each product into dense clusters while treating statistical outliers as noise. The resulting ThresholdV2 records store:

MinThreshold / MaxThreshold — the actual observed price range of the cluster (not a hand-tuned rule).
QuantityOfEAInUOM — the UOM quantity that cluster represents.
MeanEA — a per-each price anchor used when no WAP entry is available.

Because outliers are excluded, the clusters are tighter and more representative of real analyst expectations. A product that historically sells as a BX/12 at $120–$180 will produce one cluster with that range; a separate cluster covers the EA sales at $10–$15.

title: Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works and Why It Succeeds
text:
Part 2 — The Proximity Inference Formula
Once DBSCAN clusters exist, Step 5 must decide which cluster (UOM) best explains the current line’s PriceUOM. The key insight is that price operates on a log scale — a $5 difference matters much more at a $10 product than at a $10,000 product.

Log-distance calculation
logPrice = log₁₀(max(PriceUOM, 0.0001))
logLo    = log₁₀(max(MinThreshold, 0.0001))
logHi    = log₁₀(max(MaxThreshold, 0.0001))

logDist  = 0                        if  lo ≤ price ≤ hi
         = logPrice − logHi         if  price > hi   (above range)
         = logLo − logPrice         if  price < lo   (below range)
If the price falls inside the cluster band, the log-distance is exactly 0 — a perfect match. Outside the band the distance grows as the log ratio between the price and the nearest edge.


Gaussian proximity score
proximityScore = exp(−0.5 × (logDist / LOG_EPS)²)    where LOG_EPS = 0.25
This is a Gaussian kernel on log-price space. LOG_EPS = 0.25 corresponds to roughly a 1.8× price deviation from the cluster edge before the score drops to ~0.37. At a 2× deviation the score is ~0.02 — effectively zero.
Why this mirrors analyst reasoning:
Analysts verify a UOM assignment by mentally checking whether the price is “in the right ballpark” for that product and pack size. A box of 10 syringes should cost roughly 10× what one syringe costs. If the file price is 3× or 20× off, the analyst rejects it. The proximity score formalises this intuition: prices within the cluster score near 1.0, prices slightly outside drop gracefully, and wildly mismatched prices score near 0.


Four candidate pools
Step 5 evaluates up to four candidate sources and picks the one with the highest proximity score:








Source
What it is
Why it’s included




DBSCAN
Cluster from ThresholdV2
Primary signal — density-derived from history


Known Product UOM
WAP (Weighted Avg Price) from purchase history
Covers UOMs not yet in DBSCAN clusters


EA/1 WAP
Per-each derived from any known WAP ÷ quantity
Catches single-unit sales missing from DBSCAN


File UOM × WAP
File-stated UOM quantity × per-each WAP
Validates when the supplier already labelled the pack size



The File UOM candidate requires a minimum score of 0.85 to be considered — a stricter bar because file labels are not always reliable.

title: Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works and Why It Succeeds
text:
Winner-Source Breakdown


                            
                                            





Candidate
Recognitions
% of Step 5 total




DBSCAN cluster
47,554
93.6 %


Known Product UOM (WAP)
1,300
2.6 %


EA/1 WAP candidate
1,128
2.2 %


File UOM × WAP
816
1.6 %



DBSCAN is the dominant winner (93.6 %), validating that the density-based clusters are the most reliable signal. The remaining 6.4 % are split across the three supplementary pools — each of which catches a class of products the primary DBSCAN clusters do not yet cover.

title: Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works and Why It Succeeds
Deep Dive — How Step 5 Works and Why It Succeeds
text:
Why Unrecognized Rows Stay Unrecognized
Three root causes account for nearly all unrecognized rows:

1. No usable PriceUOM (5.9 % of unrecognized)
Step 5 requires a PriceUOM to compute a log-distance. Rows where PriceUOM is null or zero (3,168 rows) are immediately ineligible.


2. Low or zero purchase history


                            
                                            





Purchase history
Unrecognized rows
% of total unrecognized




Zero (no history at all)
47
0.1 %


Very low (1–4 units)
31,400
58.4 %


Some history (≥ 5 units)
21,900
40.7 %




Zero history — DBSCAN cannot form a cluster without past transactions. No ThresholdV2 exists, and no WAP can be derived. Step 5 has nothing to anchor its proximity calculation against.
Very low history (1–4 units) — a single purchase creates a technically valid cluster, but with such sparse data the cluster edges may be wildly unrepresentative. The proximity score lands below the 0.30 acceptance threshold.
Some history but low confidence — the product has enough data for DBSCAN, but the file’s PriceUOM is far enough from every cluster that no candidate scores ≥ 0.30. This typically means the supplier is quoting an unusual pack size not yet seen in history, or there is a unit-price vs pack-price mismatch in the file.



3. PriceUOM not present in purchase history
Some rows have a PriceUOM value but that price point has never appeared in the product’s purchase history. With no matching cluster and no comparable WAP anchor, the inference step cannot form a confident assignment — the proximity score for every candidate falls below 0.30 and the row exits unrecognized.

See Proposed Solutions for how these three failure modes can be addressed.

title: UOM Recognition Improvements
text:
The dataset contains 104,708 line items that were routed through the full UOM recognition pipeline. Steps 1–4 — exact-match lookups and legacy threshold rules — are fast and precise but intentionally conservative; anything they cannot confidently classify is passed to the inference step.



Metric
Count
Share




Total rows
104,708
100 %


Recognized (all steps)
50,954
48.7 %


— of which resolved by Steps 1–4
156
0.1 %


— of which resolved by Step 5
50,798
48.5 %


Unrecognized
53,754
51.3 %




Key takeaway: Steps 1–4 together account for only 0.1% of all recognitions. Step 5 is responsible for virtually the entire 48.5% recognition rate — without it, nearly all products that reach this dataset would go unmatched.

title: UOM Recognition Improvements
text:
The Role of Each Step
The UOM recognition pipeline runs five steps in order. A row exits as soon as one step successfully assigns a UOM; unmatched rows cascade to the next step.








Step
Mechanism
Strength




1
Exact UOM string match from file
Fastest, highest precision


2
Known product UOM catalogue lookup
High precision, limited coverage


3
Legacy threshold match (V1 — min/max bounds)
Broad coverage, sensitive to outliers


4
File UOM direct match
Handles file-native UOM labels


5
DBSCAN thresholds + proximity scoring
Widest coverage, probabilistic confidence



Because this dataset represents the rows that survived all four earlier steps unmatched, Step 5 carries the full weight of the remaining recognition task.

title: UOM Recognition Improvements
text:
Confidence Score Distribution (Recognized Rows)
Every row recognized by Step 5 carries a confidence score derived from its winning proximity score. A score ≥ 0.90 signals the inferred UOM is almost certainly correct; the minimum accepted is 0.30.


                            
                                            





Score band
Count
Share of Step 5 recognized




≥ 0.90 (high confidence)
38,930
76.6 %


0.70 – 0.90 (moderate)
6,402
12.6 %


0.30 – 0.70 (marginal)
5,466
10.8 %



The median confidence score is 0.99 and the mean is 0.92, confirming that the vast majority of Step 5 assignments carry strong probabilistic support.

Navigate to Deep Dive for a detailed breakdown of the inference mechanism, winner-source analysis, and why unrecognized rows remain unresolved. See Proposed Solutions for actionable next steps on zero-history products.
